insights: Expenses in the lowest cost bracket (0–1000) take 32.53 days on average to process—4.21x longer than those in the highest bracket (5000+, 7.73 days)—indicating a severe structural inefficiency that systematically penalizes small-value submissions.

The apparent inverse relationship between expense amount and processing time is fully driven by category composition: Services (slowest category, 30.71 days mean) constitute 70% of low-amount expenses, while Assets (fastest category, 13.45 days mean) make up 70% of high-amount expenses—revealing category type, not cost itself, as the true operational determinant.

Within each cost bracket, processing times show remarkably low variability (std 1.32–3.54 days), confirming that delays are not random or idiosyncratic but reflect consistent, rule-based handling patterns tied to expense type and bracket assignment.

The 0–1000 and 1000–2500 brackets collectively represent 77% of the total potential efficiency gain (4876 days), meaning targeted intervention on these two brackets would deliver the vast majority of achievable time savings and fairness improvements.

All seven departments and all four categories exhibit the same strong negative correlation between amount and processing time (r ≥ −0.9157), confirming that the pattern is organization-wide and not attributable to isolated team behavior or local policy deviations.

The 321% time penalty for smallest expenses creates a material fairness risk: employees submitting routine, low-cost operational expenses (e.g., software subscriptions, minor repairs) face disproportionately long waits compared to those submitting large capital or travel expenses—potentially undermining trust in policy equity and compliance incentives.
summary: The analysis reveals that expense processing time is strongly and inversely associated with amount—not because cost drives speed, but because low-amount expenses are overwhelmingly Services-type (30.7 days), while high-amount ones are predominantly Assets-type (13.5 days). This category-driven confounding creates a 4.21x processing time disparity across brackets, with the smallest expenses (0–1000) enduring 32.5 days on average—systematically disadvantaging routine operational spenders. Within-bracket consistency confirms this is a governed, repeatable pattern rather than noise, and 77% of recoverable processing time (4876 days) lies in the two lowest brackets. To improve both efficiency and fairness, the priority must shift from treating 'cost bracket' as a proxy for priority to directly aligning processing rules, SLAs, and automation with expense category and business intent—starting with Services-type submissions under $2500.
